common_misuse: Given to dogs or cats for fever, pain, or injury
guidance: Do not give paracetamol/acetaminophen to a dog or cat unless a veterinarian specifically prescribed it for this animal.
friendly_next_step: Keep the animal quiet and note the tablet strength, amount, and time. Call a veterinarian or poison service with those details if any was swallowed.

sources.title: Merck Veterinary Manual - Toxicoses From Human Analgesics
FDA - Potentially Dangerous Items for Your Pet

typical_use: Human pain, fever, and inflammation relief
common_misuse: Given to pets after injury or limping
guidance: Do not give ibuprofen or other human NSAIDs to dogs or cats unless a veterinarian directed it.
friendly_next_step: If it was swallowed, keep the packet and note the strength, amount, and time before calling a veterinarian.

sources.title: Merck Veterinary Manual - Toxicoses From Human Analgesics
FDA - Potentially Dangerous Items for Your Pet

typical_use: Human pain relief and anti-platelet medicine
common_misuse: Given at home for limping or pain
guidance: Do not start aspirin at home for a pet. It can interact with other medicines and can cause serious side effects without veterinary dosing and screening.
friendly_next_step: For pain or limping, restrict movement and arrange veterinary assessment rather than giving a tablet.

sources.title: Merck Veterinary Manual - Toxicoses From Human Analgesics

typical_use: Skin antiseptic and minor wound cleaning
common_misuse: Used too strong, poured into deep wounds, or used instead of veterinary wound care
guidance: For a small surface wound, diluted povidone-iodine can be used as a gentle rinse after flushing dirt away. Do not pour it into deep punctures, eyes, burns, or large wounds.
friendly_next_step: Dilute until it looks like weak tea, rinse gently, cover loosely if needed, and get help for deep, painful, dirty, or bleeding wounds.

sources.title: VCA Animal Hospitals - Povidone Iodine Topical
VCA Animal Hospitals - Care of Open Wounds in Dogs

common_misuse: Used as the only care for deep, infected, or bleeding wounds
guidance: Sterile saline is reasonable for gently flushing dirt from a minor surface wound or around the eye. Do not force fluid into deep wounds.
friendly_next_step: Flush gently, prevent licking, and arrange veterinary help if the wound is deep, painful, dirty, or not improving.
safer_alternatives: clean running water for surface dirt

sources.title: VCA Animal Hospitals - Care of Open Wounds in Dogs

typical_use: Mild fluid support after brief vomiting or diarrhea
common_misuse: Forced into vomiting, collapsed, very weak, or unconscious animals
guidance: Plain water is safest first. A pet electrolyte or unflavored oral electrolyte solution may be considered only for an alert dog who can swallow and is not vomiting repeatedly.
friendly_next_step: Offer tiny amounts slowly. If the dog is weak, repeatedly vomiting, bloody, bloated, or a puppy, call a veterinarian or rescue instead of trying to rehydrate at home.
safer_alternatives: ice chips for an alert dog

sources.title: PetMD - Can You Give a Dog Pedialyte?

common_misuse: Treating chocolate as a harmless snack for dogs
guidance: Do not feed chocolate to dogs or cats. If any was eaten, keep the wrapper and note the amount and time.
friendly_next_step: For a hypothetical question, the safe answer is no chocolate. For known ingestion, call a veterinarian or poison service with the type, amount, and time.
safer_alternatives: small amount of regular dog food

sources.title: FDA - Potentially Dangerous Items for Your Pet

guidance: Do not feed grapes or raisins to dogs or cats. They can be dangerous even when the animal looks normal at first.
friendly_next_step: If any were eaten, note the amount and time and call a veterinarian or poison service promptly.

sources.title: FDA - Potentially Dangerous Items for Your Pet

typical_use: Sweetener in sugar-free gum, candy, cough syrup, mouthwash, toothpaste, and some foods
common_misuse: Dog chews sugar-free gum or receives human toothpaste/food
guidance: Keep xylitol away from dogs. If a dog may have eaten it, treat it as urgent.
friendly_next_step: Save the packet, note the time and amount, and call a veterinarian or poison service now.

sources.title: FDA - Paws Off Xylitol; It's Dangerous for Dogs

common_misuse: Feeding leftovers cooked with onion or garlic
guidance: Avoid onion, garlic, leeks, and chives for dogs and cats, including cooked leftovers.
friendly_next_step: If a meaningful amount was eaten, note the food, amount, and time and call a veterinarian.
safer_alternatives: plain cooked rice in tiny amounts if appropriate

sources.title: FDA - Potentially Dangerous Items for Your Pet

typical_use: Human food or emergency feeding idea
common_misuse: Giving cow milk or force-feeding a cold, weak, or newly found puppy
guidance: Do not use cow milk as first aid for a cold or weak puppy, and do not force-feed.
friendly_next_step: Warm the puppy first, keep the airway clear, and seek an experienced rescuer or veterinarian if the puppy is cold, limp, crying constantly, injured, or not suckling.

sources.title: VCA Animal Hospitals - Caring for Orphaned Puppies

typical_use: Fuel, solvent, or harsh home remedy
common_misuse: Applied to wounds, maggots, ticks, or skin disease
guidance: Do not put kerosene, turpentine, petrol, diesel, or engine oil on wounds, maggots, ticks, or skin.
friendly_next_step: Prevent licking, gently rinse with clean water or saline if safe, cover loosely, and arrange vet/rescue help for open or painful wounds.

sources.title: Merck Veterinary Manual - Initial Wound Management in Small Animals
